column2: Eddy explains how taking the Nakuru county communications job led to long-distance marriage and financial strain
column3: Now, I had to supplement my salary, which had dipped after taking the county job. And like many other men would have done, I ventured into business. I ended up committing my full salary to service this loans, but the hole got even deeper. Everything I had planned to do at that time collapsed face-flat. So one thing led to another, I betrayed my marriage. I separated from my wife due to infidelity. Missed my children, and this killed me every single day and night. And I'm still trying to make up for that.

column2: Eddy's closing message to men in the Engage Kenya audience
column3: Because at the end of it all, when all is said and done, we must recognize that being perfect is a fairy tale. Failure, scarcity, and shortcomings are all part of being a man, a father, a husband. Openness, honesty, communication are key to your relationship with your partners, but sometimes, it can be difficult.
column14: Men need to be emotionally open

column2: Eddy reflects on his own conduct during the period of marriage breakdown
column3: I had done them either out of ignorance um of course ego was part of it. Again making wrong decisions was like any other man would do you make wrong decisions from time to time in life. But I didn't have the tools to to deal with all that. I did not even know there were tools to deal with that to mend that. So I just wallowed in my space that I got into.

column2: Eddy describes what stood out from his Engage talk
column3: Admitting that I failed. Accepting that I made many mistakes. Accepting and revealing that I failed my marriage. Admitting that I made wrong decisions about business. Admitting that I failed personally as a man, as a father. And I think also being an actor helped because you end up wearing masks, which is something that a lot of men are grappling with. The society is harsh on men. It's very, very harsh on men right now.

column2: Eddy describes the wave of women asking him to help their depressed husbands - and his caution
column3: I'm getting also feedback from ladies asking me to help their husbands because they feel the husband does not want to be helped. Now that one you have to be careful because again I can't start engaging somebody who may not want or is not ready to be helped. He may ask, why are you talking to my wife, what business do you have with her?

column2: Onyango explains why poverty-trauma stays with men into adulthood
column3: Mick was talking about reparenting myself and I think I need to reparent myself again about poverty because if you grow up without anything. If you are if you a friend of the bottle then whatever you do in life whether you are whatever maybe if you're a billionaire or a millionaire but yes you just remain as a family no nothing so you just grow up in lack everyday lack everyday lack everyday lack you come to the city. And I think if you get into your adulthood without luck it destroys you because you have embodied it you know it's in your body now you actually believe this is your identity. Sometimes even you can get a lot of money and it's sits in the bank and you're afraid of using it. Or you squander it because you don't believe you you you're supposed to have money.

column2: Onyango uses geography as a metaphor for why men die younger
column3: It's not a mistake that women generally live longer than men. It's not a mistake. Outside how physically testing men's lives have been, it's also because we keep all that pain inside. It's not healthy. For example, even geography, why is a salty water lake salty? Because it only has an inlet, it doesn't have an outlet. A fresh water lake is fresh because it has both inlets and outlets. Your heart has veins that pump blood in and pump blood out. If it was only inlet, we wouldn't even survive. Our brains need air. People faint because air oxygen is out from the brain. You collapse. So essentially, space is basic to everything.

column2: Onyango describes the limited emotional palette men are granted
column3: He only expresses himself when he's extremely happy or extremely sad. When he's extremely sad, he's angry, he's violent. And he's extremely happy, he's with women and alcohol and doing all kinds of nasty stuff. So, in the subconscious of a young boy, you're living in the negative energy because you do not have communities of healthy men around you. They're not there.

column2: Onyango pushes back on the trend of demanding men cry as proof of vulnerability
column3: Now even the thing about crying. There are some quarters where men have been made to feel like if you don't cry, you're not doing it enough. You're not being vulnerable enough. People have different ways of expressing pain. As long as they know, they have that space to express pain in whatever manner is comfortable to them, that is what vulnerability is. And we cannot be vulnerable according to social standards if we have not learned how to be vulnerable to our own selves.

I was raped when I was 19, I was raped when I was this. There's a story of a guy who reached out to me and he told me that he was sodomized by friends, people he knew. They sodomized him, robbed him, and he got so mad, so angry. He hired goons to go kill them. And he told me, he's sure they can't do such a thing to another person because they are rotting in their graves. Boys who are abused when they were kids. They're stuck. Pastors reach out to me. They stand on pulpits, my brother, but they're in so much pain.

column2: Philip's framing of why he is making the documentary
column3: Partnering with Equality Now, an organization committed to fighting gender based violence through legal means, I received a map, a guide to conversations that needed to be had. I set off on a journey to hear directly from survivors of sexual violence. To grasp the reality my daughter faces.

column3: (In response to Kambua noting his daughter is four years old and could be FGM-targeted within four years.) You can't wrap your head around that.

column3: How can this be a reality? How can this be our life? An 8, 9, 10-year-old child, even if it is a tradition, how do you desire such a young girl to be your wife?

column3: I think I still have a difficult time accepting some of the explanations. Because, I don't think there's a way I can look at my daughter, and one day think of her as my wife.

She told me at some point the judge actually asked her to leave the courtroom and leave the child and the perpetrator in the same room. I don't know, something about that just seems wrong. Like how do you have a child and the perpetrator in the same room at any one point? And this child is supposed to give out their statement.

column2: Eddy responds to an online discussion about men disappearing from clubs, festivals, and church events
column3: Men are not missing. Men are evolving. Men are quietly walking away from the performance culture, from chaos, from spaces that demand that they must prove their self, that they must prove their fun, that they must prove their faith or their worth. They're not hiding, they're healing. They're learning that peace is louder than music, that purpose-led life feels better than applause. To the women who may feel offended please don't be. We don't hate women. We are just learning as men to love ourselves properly so that we can love you better. We are not ghosting society. We are grounding ourselves.

Wash your hands when you leave the toilet. But we are not taught how to take care of our mental fitness our mental hygiene. So we are now in a space where we are trying to embrace and understand mental health.

column3: When you go through something traumatizing as a child, your brain could forget it. But your nervous system stores the memory. So that's why you hear somebody is taken to the hospital, maybe their body feels sickly. But there's no disease that is being found. Trauma responses manifest in very many different ways. Some people become anxious, some people overeat, some people overwork. Some people's bodies develop their immune systems go down, so they are prone to illnesses. Some people resort to drug abuse and substance. Some people are also very violent as a coping mechanism because they're trying to protect themselves. They don't want to be seen as weak.

column2: Eddy describes his rock-bottom moment after a failed county-government job, business losses, separation from wife, and being auctioned/evicted
column3: With no proper income coming through, I was kicked out of my rented house twice and got auctioned once. My friend Alan put me up the two times, and I had to do something unthinkable. At 39 years of age, I went back home, knocked on the door, and I told my mom, 'I failed.' It was extremely painful. On the surface, I kept a straight face, but deep down, I was burning. And several times, I wanted to let go.

column2: Eddy directly names the Swahili phrases used to enforce male emotional suppression
column3: Once you learn and embrace that especially for men, being weak is normal. It's good to be weak. We were raised in a society that tells us... be strong. Yes, man, brace yourself and toughen up. Men are not supposed to talk because even that idea of toughening up is repeated in different ways. You'll find situations where you want to share something with your fellow brother but you can't because you think they will... men don't do that. But that's what society has taught us. Men don't cry. Men don't show emotions. Men don't fail!
column14: Men need to be emotionally open

column2: Eddy warns of what happens to men who continue suppressing emotions
column3: We will continue having broken men. We'll continue having broken men, men who are not ready to because I also believe that men will become so numbed in struggling to keep that status that you're a man, you don't need to cry. So we've gone numb that we are not able to show feelings because we've been told by the society that you as a man is not supposed to cry. A man is not supposed to say when he's down. A man is not supposed to reach out and look for help.

column2: Eddy describes the experience of pouring out his story on the Engage stage
column3: I went onto that stage and poured my soul out. It was therapeutic. It was a load off my shoulder talking about myself and my story and what I've been through. And for me, it was a revelation because as men, we rarely talk, we rarely open up. So it was also an opportunity for me to share with like-minded men who also attend Engage and see if I could have an impact in also telling a story by hearing a story from a man and be honest and be brutal, brutal with myself.
column14: Men need to be emotionally open

column2: Onyango reflects on facilitating the workshop and what it means for Kenyan men
column3: I was the facilitator of the relate men's mental health Workshop it was one of the best days of my life why because I love talking to men I love seeing and hearing men talk to each other and today again we just witnessed how important how valuable how significant it is when men come together to talk to one another about each other. I am also quite passionate about continuing with these kind of workshops because it emulates or it models to men like me that it's actually possible to be vulnerable to one another specifically as men because it goes a long way to making us know that creating Healthy Communities with each other is one of the ways that we are going to continue feeling loved on this Earth that most days we feel very alone.
column14: Men need to be fully self-reliant
column32: Calls for men to follow more equitable gender norms

column2: Onyango's central thesis on what works most
column3: But also I must emphasize this the most sustainable tool is community community is the most sustainable tool for trauma healing because outside therapy outside all these other interventions if you do not have community to share life with it becomes very difficult to heal in isolation. The reason they exist is so that you can connect back with your body and your nervous system so that you can be able to open up again and love and share and intimate with other people because our nervous system which is the parts that are also diely affected by trauma cannot connect when they are closed up.

She don't know how to open up.

column2: Onyango places his father's violence in historical context - his grandfather fought in WWII Burma for the British
column3: I went back and I wanted to understand his childhood from a genuinely curious place. And realizing he also grew up in a violent place. And his father was a polygamist. His father, my grandpa even fought for the second, in the Second World War in Burma. And a lot of those African men who fought for the British had serious post-traumatic stress that manifested in their lives differently. Some became completely redundant, they couldn't do anything. Some became struggling alcoholics. Some, their families just wear that away.

column2: Onyango on his trauma-counseling clientele
column3: I support especially people who are battling with childhood traumas. And we are so many. You'd be surprised. We are so many from CEOs to fundi wa viatu. People battling with their relationship with their father, relationship with their mother, relationship with their siblings, relationship with their old tribe, like things people are holding in their hearts that they're struggling with. They don't have a language to express.

column2: Onyango's redefined idea of what church/community should be

Every home, every space, every space should be a safe space. Everywhere we go, we have to develop trauma-informed communities where we can humanize one another, where the first instinct when I see you and you're in trousers and a shirt, I'm not boxing you, 'Ah, you're a guy,' so I want to treat you like this because of the traumas other people other men have taken me through.

column2: Eddy responds to whether being a man is harder for the current generation
column3: It is challenged. The dynamics I mean by we are now bombarded by this whole internet and digital. It did not exist in our time. Right now there's so much pressure in terms of where they're getting information from that it might not be just one channel to pick up information. The other aspect about the girl child being too empowered. And I don't think there's anything like too empowered. Yes, they're being empowered, but I think also the boy child also has been forgotten a little bit. So there's that aspect also that is making it a challenge for boys to graduate into manhood.

column2: Eddy explains why he is using barbershops as the centerpiece for his men's mental health initiative
column3: We want to collaborate with barbershops and barbers because this is one of the few if not the only spaces that we have as men where our guards are completely down. We have very authentic conversations. We talk about sports and we get very emotional. But why not see how we can weave around this space and let one of the conversations be about our own mental health, well-being? Because it's the only place where I can comfortably sit with a man with a blade on top of my head and feel not threatened. I trust my barber.

column2: Eddy describes his depression and the moment a 'flicker' of sense came through
column3: Depression is, which is what I went through, is like a prison with no key, and the thoughts are the lock. So you keep on fighting with these thoughts constantly, in your space, that prison. I wish I knew this then. That there is a way I can do this and that is by asking for help. Saying, and raising my hand and saying, you know what? I don't think I'm okay.

column2: Eddy connects male suicide rates to economic pressures and the provider-role expectation

Because now it looks like an easy way out, but now unfortunately, some of us men are not willing to go out there and do, and toil for actual, you know, just do some honest work.

column2: Eddy explains his shift into mental health advocacy work
column3: I took my scars and turned them outwards. I trained as a mental health advocate and a trainer because it was selfish to keep holding all these hard-learned lessons. It was selfish of me through the lived experience I had.

A CEO waking up in the morning and saying I went through these tribulations. I am in touch with the problems I am and I understand what you're going through. Leaders need to start talking. About mental health.

column2: Onyango is explaining how prolonged trauma affects the brain - particularly for men exposed to constant danger or stress
column3: You can't process emotions properly and you can't think rational all right because your body is really just trying to keep you alive because you are sensing danger too often sensing danger too often get what I mean. All right this is the epitome of mental health all right because your daily experiences are connected so your body moves according to how your environment is. If you feel safe your nerves are relaxed, right; if you feel in danger your nerves are tight.

column2: Onyango explains how chronic stress in men leads to seemingly senseless violence - the bus-stop killings, the Manchester United fights
column3: When you are Overexposed to trauma it means you are constantly on survival mode trying to protect yourself. That means the part of your brain that is essential for your survival is overworking which is The Reptilian Brain. It is overworking that means the other parts of the brain's energy won't be going there for you to use. So they are affected that means you can't process emotions properly and you can't think rationally. You've heard stories of someone snaps or you hear stories on the news somebody killed the other because of some 20 Shilling debt or Manchester United Arsenal game. They can't think rationally they've been overexposed to to stress.

column2: Onyango explains why there isn't one way to heal from trauma
column3: I am really not of the idea that there is one way of dealing with trauma because you know even here we can be in one house we can may have grown up in one house but the way I respond to trauma is different from way the way you will respond to trauma. We have very different responses to to what's going on in in the moment right that also means getting out of this thing in terms of healing also looks different from for different people. So there are people who might need medical therapy because of how sometimes damaged the brain is there are people who might need just to talk about it which is talk therapy.

column2: Onyango describes his support group for male survivors of sexual abuse
column3: I have set up like a support, a safe space for men who have been sexually abused, which is another long story that I didn't share. One of the purposes I think I have in life is to support men to humanize themselves.

And, man, men from South America, men from the deepest places of this earth reaching out to me saying, you know what? I do not think I have the courage to express myself like you do in my own community. This is not just an African man's thing. This is a global man's thing. Men are struggling all over the world to talk, just talking.

column2: Joab asks Kibe why he himself got married. Kibe gives a candid answer.
column3: Yeah. I wanted to have sex. And then I was in church. So for me, it was a license to have sex. That's what people in church do. They get married so they can have sex. So that God can rubber stamp your DMs when you're having sex. That's why people get married. But take away the need for sex for a man. There's no marriage.

column2: Kibe presses Joab to justify why a man would cry at his mother's funeral - frames crying as feminine hormones
column3: I'm not a woman. My emotions don't take over. I can handle death. Why can't I handle death? Why am I being unable to process death to a point where I'm crying?

column2: Onyango's neuroscience-informed framework for breaking inherited masculine violence
column3: Your brain is a simulation machine. It repeats patterns. Yeah. Our behaviors become behaviors because they are patterns of things that were done over and over that we picked up from. That's why you see children wearing their parents' shoes. They see their parents do this. We learn from what we see, right? And because I wanted a different life for myself, for my future, for the future communities I wanted to create, it meant I had to stop my brain from simulating the violence I was taught, the violence I was conditioned to, and create a new brain for myself. And that also feels like building another world because of the energy it takes.

column2: Final closing message - Eddy directly tells men to show up for their children regardless of internal struggle
column3: After all is said and done, get close to your children. You don't have to feel strong and courageous to do this. Children need their fathers regardless what dad is going through inside. One of the gifts I'd like to give to my children is to demonstrate to them that you can recover from failure and keep moving.

column2: Eddy frames male self-care as central to mental health
column3: To be taken care of by just being self compassionate about about yourself. We need also to to to worry about ourselves. We need to take care of ourselves our well-being self care is very important.
column32: Calls for men to follow more equitable gender norms

column2: Eddy answers Eric's question about how men should support friends going through crisis
column3: It could start by having interest about your friends. And not interest about where are we going to have our next beer not having interest of where where are we going to watch the next Formula 1 race. But it could go deep down to the point where you need to you you and there are ways of doing it. We also need to be able to reciprocate to it cause I might be a very very good friend and I'm concerned about you but you do not want to open up. And there's no way I'll be your friend and you're going through something and I if I'm honest with myself as your friend not want to help you.

column2: Eddy explains why men cannot share their emotional reality with each other
column3: We are wired, of course by the society, how we've been brought up, not to show these emotions. Unfortunately, we do not know how to be vulnerable. A big general, and a very sizable number of us men do not understand how to embrace, and that's why you get the case of toxic masculinity coming into place. And you will be suppressing all these emotions. And these emotions should actually be working for us. Our vulnerability should is supposed to work for us, not against us.
column14: Men need to be emotionally open

column2: Eddy on how he made his series of bad decisions during the dark season
column3: I made wrong decisions. Wrong business decisions that's why I got into debt. I got distracted that's why my marriage collapsed. I let down my fatherhood responsibilities being away. So it was really a collapse of everything and then at the middle of it I got something called Bell's palsy. So all that all that put together everything just came tumbling down. I just I don't know how I got through it.

column2: Continuation - Onyango explaining how chronic male trauma manifests as violence and irrational behavior
column3: When you are in traumatic situations or traumatic situations for a long time it becomes chronic it means this part of your brain is overworking the part of your brain that just wants to keep you alive so it means you don't often think well or think rationally you can do things or say things that you regret or you want to be violent and stuff like that.

column2: Onyango recounts the 2015 confrontation he organized with his parents - and the pivotal statement his father made
column3: When it was my turn to talk to dad he told me things that I I think also even retraumatized me. But then as we were talking this man asked me a question that also changed my life he said I don't come to you with my problems because you think I'm weak you know and that's a very powerful statement to come from any man and that's that line sank into me like wine my friend. This man who I've lived with 25 years first of all what makes him think that I don't already know his weaknesses you see. What is this idea of him showing up as a perfect parent and he's already making the mistakes and we are suffering from them is this denial or what's going on around here?

column2: Onyango describes his ongoing confusion about his father as a person
column3: I had been asking so many questions about dad because he seemed like a very strange person to me. You know, the violence we grew up in was intensely insane.

column2: Onyango on the moment his father pivoted from search to accusation
column3: It seemed he had misplaced this text and I'm like Dad no I've I've not seen that book. He asks you this question which if an African parent asks you you have nowhere else to go. You know can the house help steal a book? Your uncle can he steal a book? You're the only person going to school here. Then he's like produce that book and it was interesting how suspicion moved to accusation.

column2: Onyango describes setting up a peer support community for people reaching out to him
column3: I set up a 200-member mental health support group online. It's just a WhatsApp group. I made a call on my socials and people reached out, like 200 people reached out. So, I opened two WhatsApp groups and I told them, 'Guys, look, nobody knows everything here. So, we are developing a community. A community that is like a village. All of us are here, all of us are important. All of us are creating content. Live as if you're living in a village, the way you'd want your village to run.'
column32: Other
column33: Live as if you're living in a village, the way you'd want your village to run.

column2: Philip's framing of the broader violence-against-women picture
column3: Over 51,000 women are killed every year by an intimate partner, or family member. That's about 140 women in a day. I quickly realised that Femicide is at the end of the long chain of SGBV. That has gone unchecked in our society for a long time.
column32: Calls for men to follow more equitable gender norms

column2: Philip responds to police reports of fathers raping their daughters
column3: I think I still have a difficult time accepting some of the explanations. Because, I don't think there's a way I can look at my daughter, and one day think of her as my wife.

column2: Philip questions community sensitization and policy enforcement
column3: So what are some of the challenges you are having in terms of, curbing this thing here locally? Judy, let me throw this back at you. Do we have enough policies? Is enough being done by our government?

column2: Philip honoring Dorcas, who runs a rescue center, before asking how she copes
column3: Of all of us who are seated here, I do not think there is anyone who has seen the horrors you have witnessed. And first of all, mentally, how do you deal with what you see every day? And finally, is there a way out? Can we put an end to FGM, and early childhood marriage?

column2: Philip pushes interviewees to imagine an unconstrained scenario for ending SGBV
column3: How can we end SGBV cases in. My question to you is, if today, money was not a problem, you have no problem with money, the law is on your side, politicians are on your side, everything is on your side. How can we end SGBV cases in Kwale?

column2: Philip's most personal moment in the finale - admitting Busia broke him
column3: What Busia has done to me, I grew up in a very difficult part of this country, in the ghetto, like in the pits. And in my head I've always told myself, I'm a hard guy. But Busia has broken me. Parts of me I didn't even know that existed. And after this conversation and the conversations I've had in Busia, I don't think I'll ever be the same again. I don't think I'll ever see the world the same again.
column17: Difficulty of growing up in Busia, a city in Kenya

column2: Jagero pushes back, pointing out women CEOs, engineers, doctors who make serious decisions. Kibe responds.
column3: I I I don't I don't know I don't know who those people are. This world is run by men. So you can get in your imagination those but they're not real. These are jobs we give them. These in in CSR. If I was honest, I would rather give that job to a man. Yes, men work harder. Men make men work harder and they work longer and they complain less. But they are extremely disagreeable, meaning they'll do a better job. Because men are disagreeable. But women are agreeable. So women will run your company into the ground.
column14: Men need to be emotionally open

column2: Joab asks how men can turn off the 'noise' in their lives. Kibe answers - his path is through confronting mothers as the source of damage.
column3: Most of most of the the damage that is done to us as human beings is done by our mothers. And that is done because there's no protection from the father. So when the father is in the picture, he's supposed to protect the the children against what the mom would do to the kids. So you find kids who are raised by their mom alone without the presence of the father or at least the influence of the father. They tend to hate both parents. So you start first with your mother because your mother is one who is in direct contact with you. But once you forgive her, then you realize you've been carrying around all this for all these years, yet your biggest bully was your mother.

column2: Kibe continues pushing his argument about gender differences - extending it from physical sports to all domains
column3: The the place where women excel is in getting babies, okay? And raising them. That's the only thing the man cannot do. Everything else the woman falls short of what the man can do. It doesn't matter which discipline. It doesn't matter what it is you say. Show me one sport where women do better than men.

column2: Joab reads from Kibe's book - 'Indecision is weakness' - and asks what's wrong with men being raised in 'an environment of feelings.'
column3: You could tell the boy who was coming from who was a mama's boy. The word mama's boy existed for a reason. He was pumpered by the mother. You can be pumpered, but when at the playground, you're a beast. You're weak. You're a beast. But this guy in the playground, he's weak. He even plays with the girls. Because of who? Because of mama.

column2: Joab asks 'What is this marriage?' and whether men couldn't find their casual sexual partner in their wife. Kibe rejects this.
column3: Impossible. Because you guys are spending too much time together. And then once she starts getting babies, how can she go back to being a young woman? Now she's been elevated to mother. Now you have to look for a— you jump from wife, mother of my kids, you get another young woman, she becomes mother of your kids, get another hole. You know that is the life of a man.

column2: Kibe's claim about why marriage is unsafe for men in modern Western societies
column3: Your wife decides to fuck you, you're done. There's nothing you can do. It doesn't matter how many muscles you have how many PhDs, how many degrees, how much money you have. The society does not protect you anymore. In fact, in societies like America, it is actually out to fix the man. If the man attack, shoot ah the wife says, 'Oh, this guy is abusing me with his fats.'

column2: Joab asks what a society without marriage looks like - Kibe points to Black America as the existing example
column3: America. Have you gone to black America? The black community? That's exactly what you don't have to go to 2050. It's happening today. That's what's happening. Chaos. It's full chaos where the women are in charge of the kids. Full chaos. Full is war and fight and everything.
